title: Log-integrability of Rademacher Fourier series and applications to random analytic functions
speaker: Alon Nishry
affiliation: Institute for Advanced Study; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: We prove that the logarithm of Fourier series with random signs is integrable to any positive power. We use this result to prove the angular equidistribution of the zeros of entire functions with random signs (and more generally the almost sure convergence of certain linear statistics of the zeros).

proof_stages.label: Jensen-type zero-counting reduction
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by expressing smoothed zero counts of an analytic function in a disk through boundary averages of log |f|, so that random zero-counting questions reduce to logarithmic integrability of the random boundary values.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian benchmark and sign obstruction
proof_stages.summary: For complex Gaussian coefficients the pointwise logarithmic expectation is explicitly computable from the variance, while Rademacher signs can create atoms at zero and make pointwise logarithmic expectations unusable.

proof_stages.label: Uniform log-integrability theorem for Rademacher Fourier series
proof_stages.summary: The central theorem is stated: normalized Rademacher Fourier series have uniformly bounded logarithmic integrability moments, with a slightly more general version allowing a controlled random constant and with bounds independent of the deterministic coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Applications to random analytic functions
proof_stages.summary: The log-integrability result is applied to answer Kac's question on Rademacher analytic functions in the disk and to obtain sharper zero-distribution results such as angular equidistribution for random analytic functions with sign coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Product-space small-value reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is reformulated on the product of the probability space and the circle as a small-ball estimate for the set where the Rademacher Fourier series is tiny, and then reduced to a general distribution inequality over arbitrary measurable subsets.

proof_stages.label: Large-set estimate via an off-diagonal operator
proof_stages.summary: For sets of large measure, the restriction norm is controlled by expanding the quadratic form into diagonal and off-diagonal parts, estimating the associated operator through Hilbert-Schmidt/Bessel bounds and improved bilinear Khintchine inequalities.

proof_stages.label: Small-set enlargement and Turán-Nazarov mechanism
proof_stages.summary: For very small sets, the proof iteratively enlarges the set while preserving control of the restriction norm, using one-dimensional sections, Turán-Nazarov inequalities for exponential polynomials, and local approximation by random exponential polynomials.

examples.name: Complex Gaussian coefficient case
examples.note: The Gaussian random analytic series is used as the easy benchmark where the pointwise expectation of the logarithm reduces to a deterministic variance term plus a universal constant, highlighting by contrast what becomes hard for signs.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the pointwise law and variance of the complex Gaussian series, normalizes it, evaluates the expected logarithm up to a constant, and uses this to discuss zero counts

passes.reason: Complex Gaussian coefficients form the rotationally symmetric exactly solvable baseline, not an obstruction.
The complex Gaussian coefficient model is used as the tractable baseline where log-expectations are computable, with rotationally regular Gaussian structure but not as an obstruction.
The complex Gaussian coefficient case is used as the tractable benchmark, with circular Gaussian symmetry making the pointwise logarithmic expectation computable.
iid complex Gaussian coefficients form the rotationally/unitarily symmetric benchmark case used for a direct computation, not to refute a claim
The complex Gaussian coefficient model is rotationally invariant and is used as the easy benchmark where the logarithmic expectation is computable.

examples.name: Rademacher polynomial that vanishes with positive probability
examples.note: The possibility that a plus-minus-one polynomial vanishes at a point makes the logarithmic expectation infinite there, forcing the move from pointwise estimates to integrated logarithmic control.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only invokes the possibility that a plus-minus-one polynomial may vanish with positive probability, causing the logarithm to blow up, without constructing or analyzing a specific polynomial

passes.reason: A Rademacher polynomial with a positive-probability zero is used to show pointwise log expectations can blow up.
A Rademacher polynomial with a positive-probability zero is invoked to show the pointwise logarithmic-expectation/Fubini argument can fail.
A Rademacher polynomial with an atom at zero is invoked to show the pointwise logarithmic expectation/Fubini approach can fail.
an ordinary Rademacher polynomial with an atom at zero is used to show the pointwise expectation-of-log argument can fail
The Rademacher polynomial is used to show that pointwise vanishing with positive probability can make the log expectation blow up and obstruct the naive Fubini argument.
A discrete Rademacher polynomial can have an atom at zero, showing the pointwise logarithmic expectation/Fubini argument can fail.
used to show the pointwise logarithmic expectation/Fubini argument can fail because a Rademacher polynomial may hit zero with positive probability
Used to show the naive pointwise expectation of the logarithm can fail when a Rademacher polynomial has an atom at zero.
Used as an atom-at-zero obstruction showing the pointwise expectation of the logarithm can blow up for Rademacher coefficients.
Obstructs the pointwise log-expectation/Fubini argument because an ordinary finite Rademacher sum can hit zero with positive probability.
It is used to show pointwise logarithmic expectations can blow up when a discrete Rademacher value has an atom at zero.
Used to show the pointwise logarithmic expectation can fail when a Rademacher polynomial has a zero atom.

examples.name: Bad random constant obstruction
examples.note: Choosing the added random constant to cancel the nonconstant part gives a degenerate constant-function situation, explaining why the generalized theorem needs a technical condition on the random constant.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the bad random-constant case to show why an extra condition is needed, explaining that the series can collapse to a constant-function obstruction

passes.reason: The arbitrary random constant collapses the Fourier series to a theta-independent obstruction to the unrestricted statement.
The bad theta-independent random constant is a collapsed Fourier-series case used as an obstruction showing extra conditions on the added constant are necessary.
The bad random constant collapses the Fourier series to a constant function and is used as the obstruction requiring extra hypotheses.
the adversarial random constant collapses the Fourier series to a constant/zero-type case and motivates the needed side condition
The arbitrary random constant collapses the Fourier series to a theta-independent constant and is presented as the bad case showing an added condition is necessary.
The bad random constant can collapse the Fourier series to a pure constant case, obstructing an unconditional theorem.
used as an obstruction to dropping the condition on the random constant, producing a collapsed constant-type case
Used as an obstruction to an unconstrained theorem, and the resulting theta-independent constant function is collapsed to the zero-frequency case.
A theta-independent constant case is invoked as a collapsed bad case showing why an extra condition on the random constant is needed.
Obstructs an unrestricted random-constant theorem by collapsing the Fourier dependence to a theta-independent constant.
The random constant choice collapses the Fourier series to a constant obstruction to any theorem without the added condition.
Used as an obstruction to dropping the condition on the random constant, and structurally collapses to a θ-independent constant function.

examples.name: Lacunary Fourier series analogy
examples.note: Lacunary Fourier series are cited as the model source for the small-subspace intuition behind the operator estimates for Rademacher Fourier series.
examples.engagement_reason: lacunary Fourier series are cited only as an analogy/source of proof ideas for small subspaces, with no concrete lacunary example worked through

passes.reason: A lacunary Fourier series has a sparse gapped spectrum and is used only as an analogy for a small subspace.
